title: Martial law, authoritarian rule and Cha-cha
sub_topics: constitutional limits on martial law declaration
rebellion or invasion as sole grounds
congressional and Supreme Court review of ML
72-hour detention rule under suspension of habeas corpus
coup d'etat as path to authoritarianism
abolition of presidency / Congress / Senate under Cha-cha

stances.claim: Martial law is not evil per se — the 1987 Constitution has 'exorcised the evils' of the Marcos-era ML through safeguards: it can be declared only to suppress actual rebellion or invasion, is subject to congressional and SC review, and the Bill of Rights remains in full force.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional textualism + structural contrast with Marcos-era ML

stances.claim: The real danger is authoritarian rule — when the Constitution is ignored and the head of state governs alone, ruling by might rather than right; this destroys the Bill of Rights and abolishes Congress and the Supreme Court.

He withholds judgment on Charter change to federalism until specific details are known — federalism would be 'as novel as a revolutionary government' for a country that has never had decentralized rule since Spanish colonization.
stances.rhetorical_move: hedged position pending information + historical framing

events: 45th anniversary of Marcos martial law declaration (Sept 21, 2017)

signature_phrases: ML is not evil per se
rule by might, not by right
Neither yes nor no to Cha-cha for now
register_markers: as I have written in the past
But to be fair, let me add
Section headers with explicit yes/no/neither framing (rhetorical structuring device)
decision_framework_signals: applies constitutional textualism to distinguish constitutionally-bounded ML from extra-constitutional authoritarianism
frames safeguards (legislative + judicial review, Bill of Rights non-suspension) as the operative test for legitimacy
uses information-conditional reasoning ('I cannot say yes or no until details are known') for major structural reforms
cross_references: as I have written in the past
notable_anecdotes: Defense Secretary Lorenzana being 'a gentleman determined to honor these limits'
